_meta.posterior_method: Approximate Bayesian importance sampling (n_draws=10000) over DerSimonian-Laird τ; weakly-informative priors per pre-registered protocol (μ~N(0,0.5), τ~Half-Normal(0,0.3))
_meta.interpretation: d ≈ 0.2 small, 0.5 medium, 0.8 large. P(μ>0) is posterior probability the population effect is positive. Status flags: DOMINANT/STRONG/MODEST/FRAGILE/DESERT reflect the size of the underlying inclusion literature, not the magnitude of any one effect.
_meta.k_threshold_note: Posteriors are reported only for techniques with k≥2 meta-eligible studies. The 6 techniques with current posteriors are the Atlas v0.1 pilot; 14 more techniques are projected to cross k≥5 in Phase 2 expansion. The 15 'desert' techniques have ZERO peer-reviewed studies meeting inclusion criteria — this is the Atlas's most counterintuitive finding.

techniques.name: Gain framing
techniques.definition: Emphasize positive outcomes of the desired behavior ("you'll save X") rather than negative consequences of not acting.
techniques.when_to_use: Customer is curious / inbound. Activation moment. First-touch on a warm prospect.
techniques.when_to_avoid: Buyer is already considering a switch (loss framing tends to outperform there).

techniques.name: Loss-aversion framing
techniques.definition: Emphasize negative consequences of NOT adopting the behavior.
techniques.when_to_use: Buyer is dissatisfied with status quo. Already evaluating alternatives.
techniques.when_to_avoid: Cold first-touch — can read as fear-mongering.
techniques.example_skeleton: Most teams in your situation are leaving <quantified loss> on the table by staying on <status quo>.

techniques.name: Regulatory fit
techniques.definition: Match message framing to the recipient's regulatory orientation (promotion-focused → gain framing; prevention-focused → loss/security framing). The 'fit' itself produces additional persuasive lift beyond the framing alone.
techniques.when_to_use: When you have prior signal on the buyer's regulatory orientation (e.g. CFO/risk-mgmt → prevention; CEO/growth → promotion).
techniques.when_to_avoid: Cold first-touch with no buyer signal.
techniques.example_skeleton: If CFO/risk: '<product> reduces <named risk> from X to Y.' If CEO/growth: '<product> unlocks <named upside>.'

techniques.name: Extreme anchor
techniques.definition: Establish a high (or low) reference point so the subsequent ask feels small (or large) by contrast.
techniques.when_to_use: Pricing conversation. Negotiation opener. Already in dialogue.
techniques.when_to_avoid: Cold first-touch subject line — reads as a stunt.
techniques.example_skeleton: Most teams your size end up at <high anchor>/yr on this stack. We'd start you at <real ask>.

techniques.name: Social proof
techniques.definition: Cite peers or similar entities who have already adopted.
techniques.when_to_use: Mid-funnel. Buyer is uncertain about category fit and looking for de-risking signal.
techniques.when_to_avoid: Buyer self-identifies as a category-of-one (rare founders, contrarians). Cold first-touch where the named peers aren't recognizable.
techniques.example_skeleton: <N> of the <segment> we work with switched from <competitor>. Common reason: <specific gap>.
techniques.caveats: High-heterogeneity outlier (τ_median=0.846); CrI does not cleanly exclude zero. Treat as suggestive, not settled.

techniques.name: Commitment & consistency
techniques.definition: Secure a small, public commitment that aligns with the larger ask. The buyer's drive for self-consistency then pulls them toward the larger commitment.
techniques.when_to_use: Discovery → demo handoff. Small ask first (15-min call, single-page brief), then escalate.
techniques.when_to_avoid: When the small ask doesn't naturally chain to the larger one (manipulative if forced).
techniques.example_skeleton: Quick 15 minutes to confirm <named pain> applies to your team. If it doesn't, we both move on.

techniques_without_posteriors.name: Foot-in-the-door
techniques_without_posteriors.note: k≥5 inclusion records; posterior pending Phase 2 expansion
